guide_description: Three days covering Prague's essential triad: the medieval Old Town and its astronomical clock, the castle hill and baroque Lesser Town on the west bank, and the Jewish Quarter alongside the New Town's grand boulevard. Each day is geographically self-contained and walkable without a metro.

days.cluster_name: Old Town and the Royal Route
days.cluster_tagline: Medieval squares, Gothic towers, and Europe's most famous astronomical clock
days.neighborhood_description: The right-bank historic core that UNESCO inscribed in 1992. Narrow lanes radiating from the Old Town Square hold Romanesque cellars, Gothic churches, and baroque palaces stacked on top of one another across nine centuries. The Royal Route cuts west through the quarter toward Charles Bridge.

morning.activity_title: Old Town Square and Astronomical Clock
morning.notes: Arrive before 9am to see the square before tour groups arrive. The Orloj (astronomical clock) chimes on the hour with a mechanical procession of the Twelve Apostles - the show lasts 45 seconds and the crowd is 10 deep by 10am, so the morning opening at 9am is the cleanest viewing. Climb the Old Town Hall tower for the best aerial view of the square's medieval roofline - the entrance is inside the hall building on the south side of the tower.

walk_to_next.to: Church of Our Lady before Tyn

afternoon.activity_title: Church of Our Lady before Tyn
afternoon.notes: The twin Gothic spires dominating the Old Town Square skyline are 80 meters tall and asymmetrical by design - the south tower is wider than the north. The interior holds the tomb of Tycho Brahe, the Danish astronomer who died in Prague in 1601. Enter from the Tyn Courtyard arcade on the north side, not from the square itself where the passage is easy to miss.

evening.notes: The 30 baroque statues lining the bridge are largely 17th and 18th-century originals now replaced by replicas - the originals are in the Lapidarium museum. Cross to the Mala Strana side and look back east at dusk: the Old Town Bridge Tower frames the view and the castle hill catches the last light above the river. The bridge is pedestrian-only but extremely crowded midday; dusk thins the crowd noticeably.

if_time_permits.notes: Peter Parler's 14th-century Gothic tower at the Old Town end of Charles Bridge. The climb gives a view directly down the bridge and back across the Old Town rooftops that no other viewpoint replicates. Entrance fee is modest; the stairs are steep and uneven.

One of the best-preserved 18th-century theatres in Europe and the venue of Don Giovanni's world premiere in 1787, conducted by Mozart himself. The neo-classical facade on Ovocny trh is modest but the auditorium interior - viewable on guided tours - is a mint-green and gold jewel. Check the box office for evening performances, which are the real reason to visit.

days.cluster_tagline: A hilltop fortress complex above baroque lanes and a city of red rooftops
days.neighborhood_description: The left-bank quarter defined by Prague Castle above and the baroque Lesser Town below. Hradcany is essentially the castle precinct and surrounding aristocratic palaces; Mala Strana is the dense wedge of cobbled streets, embassy gardens, and churches tucked between the castle hill and the river.

morning.notes: The largest ancient castle complex in the world by area - it is a self-contained district, not a single building. Buy the Circuit B ticket at the main box office inside the second courtyard for St. Vitus Cathedral, the Old Royal Palace, the Basilica of St. George, and Golden Lane. Enter through the western Hradcanske namesti gate rather than the tourist-clogged main east gate off Hradcanska metro for a quieter start. The changing of the guard happens at noon on the hour at the first courtyard.

afternoon.notes: Started in 1344 and not completed until 1929 - the nave is Gothic, the rose window Art Nouveau, the chapel of St. Wenceslas covered in 1400 semi-precious stones. The Alfons Mucha Art Nouveau stained-glass window in the third chapel on the left is worth stopping for specifically: it was commissioned by a Czech insurance company in 1931. Included with the castle circuit ticket.

evening.notes: Albrecht von Wallenstein's 17th-century baroque garden behind his enormous Mala Strana palace - now the Czech Senate, so the garden is publicly accessible but the palace itself is not always open. The bronze statues along the main allée are copies; the Dutch originals were looted by Swedish troops in 1648 and are now in Drottningholm. Free entry, closes at 7pm in summer. Wander south afterward through the Mala Strana lanes toward Maltezske namesti for the quietest part of the quarter.

if_time_permits.notes: A row of tiny 16th-century houses built into the castle wall originally housing the castle guard. Franz Kafka lived at number 22 from 1916 to 1917 - his sister rented it, and he used it as a writing studio. The interiors are now small museum installations. Included with the castle circuit ticket.

A one-fifth scale steel replica of the Eiffel Tower built for the 1891 Prague Jubilee Exhibition. Take the funicular from Ujezd street rather than hiking up - it runs on a counterweight water system. On a clear day the view from the top takes in the entire Vltava basin to the Bohemian hills.

days.cluster_tagline: The former Jewish ghetto, Art Nouveau architecture, and the city's grand civic boulevard
days.neighborhood_description: Josefov is the former Jewish quarter of Prague, one of the best-preserved medieval Jewish urban districts in Europe, packed into six compact blocks north of the Old Town Square. A 15-minute walk south leads to the New Town and Wenceslas Square, the wide boulevard that served as the stage for the 1989 Velvet Revolution.

morning.activity_title: Old Jewish Cemetery and Pinkas Synagogue
morning.notes: Active from the 15th century until 1787, with up to 12 layers of graves stacked because the community could not bury outside the ghetto walls - the crowded, tilted headstones are a direct physical record of that constraint. The Pinkas Synagogue next door has the names of 77,297 Bohemian and Moravian Jewish Holocaust victims inscribed on its interior walls. Combined ticket with the Jewish Museum covers all six sites in the quarter. Morning visit before 10am is significantly quieter.

afternoon.notes: Built in 1868 in Moorish Revival style, the interior is the most visually striking of all the Josefov synagogues - gilded geometric ornament covering every wall surface. Named for the Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain in 1492 who settled in Prague. Included in the combined Jewish Museum ticket. Franz Kafka attended Hebrew school in a building on the adjacent square.

evening.notes: Not a square in the classic sense but a 750-meter boulevard sloping uphill to the neo-Renaissance National Museum. The equestrian statue of St. Wenceslas at the top was where Czechoslovak independence was proclaimed in 1918 and where Vaclav Havel addressed hundreds of thousands during the Velvet Revolution in November 1989 - a plaque on the pavement near the statue marks where Jan Palach self-immolated in 1969 in protest of the Soviet occupation. Walk the full length to the museum facade for the city view back down the boulevard.

if_time_permits.notes: The finest Art Nouveau building in Prague, completed in 1912 on the site of a demolished royal palace. Every interior surface was designed by a different Czech artist - Alfons Mucha painted the mayor's reception salon. Walk in for the free ground-floor public areas including the cafe; guided tours of the upper halls are offered several times daily and are worth the price for access to the Mucha rooms.

Built around 1270, it is the oldest surviving synagogue in Europe and still an active place of worship. The brick Gothic interior with its distinctive twin-nave vaulting is deliberately undecorated - Jewish law prohibited figural imagery. Separate ticket from the Jewish Museum complex. The attic is said to contain the remains of the Golem of Prague, a figure of Jewish folklore associated with the 16th-century Rabbi Loew.
